senses.definition: Official decisions or judgments made by a court or other authority.
examples: The lawyer reviewed past adjudgments before preparing the appeal.
Several recent adjudgments have changed how this law is applied.

senses.definition: Matters or issues that have already been finally decided by a court.
examples: The claim was dismissed as adjudicata and could not be heard again.
Once matters are adjudicata, the parties must accept the outcome.

senses.definition: To make an official decision about a problem or dispute, especially in a court or competition.
examples: The panel will adjudicate the dispute between the two companies.
An independent body was appointed to adjudicate on the complaint.
The court will adjudicate whether the contract is valid.

senses.definition: Decided officially by a judge or other authority.
examples: The case was adjudicated in less than six months.
All claims have now been fully adjudicated.

senses.definition: Makes an official decision about a problem or dispute.
examples: The tribunal adjudicates hundreds of cases every year.
The committee adjudicates on all disciplinary matters.

senses.definition: Making an official decision about a problem or dispute.
examples: She is responsible for adjudicating disputes between employees.
The board is adjudicating on several complex claims.

senses.definition: The process of making an official decision about a problem or dispute, especially in a court or competition.
examples: The dispute will go to independent adjudication if no agreement is reached.
Adjudication can be a faster alternative to traditional court proceedings.
The parties agreed to binding adjudication.

senses.definition: Official decisions or judgments reached through an adjudication process.
examples: The council publishes summaries of all adjudications on its website.
Several adjudications have clarified how the rules should be applied.

senses.definition: Relating to making official decisions about disputes or legal cases.
examples: The committee has an adjudicative role within the organization.
Adjudicative powers are given to certain regulatory bodies.

senses.definition: A person or official who makes an official decision about a problem or dispute.
examples: An independent adjudicator was appointed to hear the case.
The adjudicator decided in favor of the employee.
If you are unhappy with the decision, you can write to the adjudicator.

senses.definition: People or officials who make official decisions about problems or disputes.
examples: The panel of adjudicators listened carefully to both sides.
Experienced adjudicators are needed for complex legal cases.

senses.definition: Having the power or responsibility to make official decisions about disputes or legal cases.
examples: The agency performs both regulatory and adjudicatory functions.
Adjudicatory hearings will be held later this year.

senses.definition: The system, process, or act of judging and giving legal decisions.
examples: The reform aims to modernize the country's adjudicature.
He wrote a book on the history of the English adjudicature.

senses.definition: In linear algebra, the matrix formed by taking the transpose of the cofactor matrix of a given square matrix.
examples: The inverse of the matrix can be found using its adjugate.
Students learned how to compute the adjugate of a 3×3 matrix.

senses.definition: A very rare or obsolete word, probably referring to assistance or something that helps.
examples: The term 'adjument' appears only in a few historical texts.
He treated the old word 'adjument' as a synonym for aid.

senses.definition: Something added to another thing but not essential to it.
examples: Online materials are used as an adjunct to the main textbook.
The therapy is often an adjunct to medication.

senses.definition: A person who works in a supporting or temporary role, especially a teacher at a college or university.
examples: She works as an adjunct at the local university.
The department hired several adjuncts to cover first-year courses.

senses.definition: Added or attached in a subordinate or non-essential role.
examples: He was appointed adjunct director of the program.
Adjunct staff support the core teaching team.

senses.definition: The act of adding or joining something to something else.
examples: The adjunction of new clauses changed the meaning of the contract.
Adjunction of further conditions will require board approval.

senses.definition: Acts of adding or joining things, or things that have been added or joined.
examples: Several adjunctions were made to the original agreement.
The lawyer pointed out that these adjunctions had not been approved.

senses.definition: Used together with something else, usually as a secondary or supporting treatment.
examples: The drug is used as adjunctive therapy for depression.
Adjunctive treatments can improve recovery rates.

senses.definition: In a way that supports or adds to something else, usually as a secondary treatment.
examples: The medication is used adjunctively with psychotherapy.
These techniques are applied adjunctively rather than alone.

senses.definition: In an added or subordinate way, as something not central.
examples: He served adjunctly on the committee during the review.
The service operates adjunctly to the main program.

senses.definition: Things that are added to something else but are not essential to it.
examples: These workshops are useful adjuncts to the main training course.
In many colleges, adjuncts play an important role in teaching.

senses.definition: A serious and urgent request, often made in a formal or emotional way.
examples: Despite her adjuration, he refused to change his mind.
The letter ended with an adjuration to remain calm.

senses.definition: Serious and urgent requests, often made in a formal or emotional way.
examples: Her repeated adjurations were ignored by the crowd.
The speech was full of adjurations to stay united.

senses.definition: Expressing a serious and urgent request.
examples: His adjuratory tone showed how desperate he was.
The letter had an adjuratory style throughout.

senses.definition: To ask someone very seriously and strongly to do something.
examples: I adjure you to tell the truth.
She adjured him not to waste this opportunity.

senses.definition: Asked someone very seriously and strongly to do something.
examples: The witness was adjured to speak honestly.
He adjured them not to lose hope.

senses.definition: A person who earnestly and seriously urges someone to do something.
examples: The adjurer begged the crowd to remain peaceful.
As an adjurer, he appealed to their sense of justice.

senses.definition: People who seriously and urgently beg others to do something.
examples: The adjurers called on the leaders to end the violence.
Throughout history, adjurers have tried to persuade rulers to act justly.

senses.definition: Seriously and strongly asks someone to do something.
examples: He adjures the audience to think carefully about their choices.
The speaker adjures us not to ignore the warning signs.

senses.definition: Seriously and strongly asking someone to do something.
examples: She stood before the crowd, adjuring them to remain calm.
Adjuring the jury, the lawyer asked them to consider the evidence carefully.

senses.definition: Someone who adjures, or seriously urges another person to do something.
examples: The adjuror appealed to their conscience.
As an adjuror, she spoke with great emotion.

senses.definition: People who seriously urge others to do something.
examples: The adjurors gathered outside the court and begged for mercy.
Throughout the campaign, adjurors called for peace.

senses.definition: To change something slightly so that it works better, fits better, or is more suitable.
examples: You should adjust the seat before you start driving.
We had to adjust our plans because of the weather.
She adjusted the volume on the radio.

senses.definition: To get used to a new situation by changing your behavior or attitude.
examples: It took him a while to adjust to life in a new country.
Children adjust quickly to new environments.

senses.definition: The qualities of things that can be changed or adjusted easily.
examples: The different adjustabilities of the machine parts make it very flexible.
Designers considered the adjustabilities of the chair's features.

senses.definition: The ability of something to be changed or adjusted easily.
examples: The adjustability of the chair makes it comfortable for many users.
Good adjustability is an important feature of modern equipment.

senses.definition: Able to be changed or moved so that it works better or fits better.
examples: The desk has an adjustable height.
These goggles come with an adjustable strap.

senses.definition: In a way that allows changes to be made easily.
examples: The shelves are adjustably mounted on the wall.
The device can be adjustably configured to suit different needs.

senses.definition: An old or rare term referring to a fee or cost for adjustment or settlement.
examples: The merchant paid an adjustage on the goods.
Historical documents mention an adjustage charged on the cargo.

senses.definition: A rare word meaning the act of adjusting or settling something.
examples: The adjustation of the accounts took several days.
Old contracts refer to the adjustation of losses.

senses.definition: Changed something slightly so that it worked better, fitted better, or was more suitable.
examples: She adjusted the mirror before driving.
We adjusted our schedule to avoid the traffic.

senses.definition: Changed to take something into account or to show something more accurately.
examples: The report shows the adjusted figures for inflation.
Adjusted earnings were higher than expected.

senses.definition: A device or part that is used to change or control the position or level of something.
examples: The chair has a height adjuster on the side.
You can use the adjuster to change the brightness.

senses.definition: A person whose job is to assess damage or loss, especially for an insurance company.
examples: The insurance adjuster inspected the damage to the car.
An adjuster will contact you within three days.

senses.definition: Devices or parts used to change or control the position or level of something.
examples: The helmet straps have small adjusters on them.
Modern cars have electronic adjusters for the seats.

senses.definition: People who assess damage or loss, especially for insurance companies.
examples: The company sent two adjusters to evaluate the damage.
Claims adjusters often travel to inspect accident sites.

senses.definition: Changing something slightly so that it works better, fits better, or is more suitable.
examples: She is adjusting the chair to a comfortable height.
They are adjusting the program to meet users' needs.

senses.definition: Getting used to a new situation by changing behavior or attitude.
examples: He is still adjusting to his new job.
The children are adjusting well to school life.

senses.definition: Serving to adjust or intended to make something fit or work better.
examples: He performed several adjustive movements on the machine.
Adjustive measures were taken to improve the system.

senses.definition: A small change made to something so that it works better, fits better, or is more suitable.
examples: After a few minor adjustments, the machine worked perfectly.
You may need to make some adjustments to the settings.
The dentist made a slight adjustment to the braces.

senses.definition: The process of becoming more familiar with a new situation.
examples: Moving to a new city requires a period of adjustment.
His adjustment to university life was quite smooth.

senses.definition: A change in the amount of money paid or owed, especially so that it is fair or correct.
examples: You may receive an adjustment to your bill next month.
The company made an adjustment for inflation in the report.
collocations: make an adjustment to the bill

senses.definition: Relating to or involving adjustment.
examples: The report focused on the adjustmental changes in behavior.
Adjustmental difficulties are common after major life events.

senses.definition: Small changes made to things so that they work better, fit better, or are more suitable.
examples: Several adjustments were needed before the system ran smoothly.
After a few adjustments, the dress fitted perfectly.

senses.definition: Another spelling of 'adjuster', especially for a person who assesses damage or loss for an insurance company.
examples: The insurance adjustor visited the house after the flood.
An adjustor will evaluate your claim.

senses.definition: A rare or nonstandard plural form of 'adjustor', meaning people who assess damage or loss for an insurance company.
examples: Several adjustores met to discuss the complex claim.
Insurance adjustores examined the site together.
